State Pension.d: Contributory pension paid to individuals who have reached State Pension age and have sufficient National Insurance contribution records. Comprises the new State Pension (from April 2016, full rate Â£221.20/week in 2024-25) and the legacy basic/additional State Pension for those who reached pension age before 6 April 2016.

NHS England Core Revenue Allocation.d: Core revenue funding for NHS England covering hospital and community health services, primary care (GP contracts, prescribing, dental, ophthalmic), specialised commissioning, and public health services delegated from DHSC. This is the single largest DEL programme in UK government.
NHS England Core Revenue Allocation.b: Approximately 56.5 million people registered with NHS GPs in England

Debt Interest Payments (National Debt Se.d: Interest and financing costs on UK central government debt, including conventional gilts, index-linked gilts, National Savings & Investments products, and Treasury bills. A significant portion relates to index-linked gilt coupons that rise with RPI inflation.
Debt Interest Payments (National Debt Se.b: Not applicable (payments to gilt holders, institutional investors, NS&I savers)

Dedicated Schools Grant (DSG).d: Ring-fenced grant to local authorities in England for funding mainstream schools, academies, and free schools. Comprises four blocks: Schools Block (~Â£45B), High Needs Block (~Â£10B for SEND), Early Years Block (~Â£4B), and Central Schools Services Block.
Dedicated Schools Grant (DSG).b: Approximately 8.9 million pupils in state-funded schools in England

Universal Credit.d: Means-tested benefit replacing six legacy benefits (income-based JSA, income-related ESA, Income Support, Housing Benefit, Child Tax Credit, Working Tax Credit) into a single monthly payment for working-age people on low income or out of work.
Universal Credit.b: Approximately 6.0 million households on Universal Credit (2024-25)

Personal Independence Payment (PIP).d: Non-means-tested, non-contributory benefit for working-age adults (16-State Pension age) with long-term health conditions or disabilities to help with extra costs of daily living and mobility. Assessed via functional capability rather than diagnosis.

Housing Benefit.d: Means-tested benefit to help tenants on low incomes pay their rent, covering both social housing and private rented sector (subject to Local Housing Allowance rates). Being gradually replaced by the housing element of Universal Credit.
Housing Benefit.b: Approximately 2.8 million claimants (declining as UC migration proceeds)

Employment and Support Allowance (ESA).d: Benefit for people with a health condition or disability that limits their ability to work. Comes in two forms: contributory (based on NI record, now called ’new style’ ESA) and income-related (legacy, being replaced by UC). Claimants placed in Support Group or Work-Related Activity Group.
Employment and Support Allowance (ESA).b: Approximately 1.7 million claimants (declining due to UC migration)

Pension Credit.d: Means-tested top-up benefit for pensioners on low incomes, guaranteeing a minimum weekly income (Guarantee Credit, Â£218.15/week for singles in 2024-25). Also includes a Savings Credit element rewarding modest retirement savings for those who reached pension age before April 2016.
Pension Credit.b: Approximately 1.4 million claimant households (significant under-claiming estimated at ~850,000 eligible non-claimants)

Attendance Allowance.d: Non-means-tested, tax-free benefit for people of State Pension age and above who have a physical or mental disability requiring frequent attention or supervision. Paid at two rates: lower (Â£72.65/week daytime or nighttime needs) and higher (Â£108.55/week both).

Child Benefit.d: Universal (non-means-tested) payment to parents or guardians responsible for a child under 16 (or under 20 in approved education/training). Paid at Â£25.60/week for eldest child and Â£16.95/week for each additional child (2024-25 rates).
Child Benefit.b: Approximately 7.0 million families covering 12.7 million children

Tax Credits (Working Tax Credit and Chil.d: Means-tested income supplements for working people on low incomes (Working Tax Credit) and families with children (Child Tax Credit). Legacy benefits being replaced by Universal Credit through managed migration.
Tax Credits (Working Tax Credit and Chil.b: Approximately 1.5 million households (rapidly declining from peak of 4.4 million in 2012)

Police Core Grant and Formula Funding.d: Central government grants to the 43 territorial police forces in England and Wales, comprising the Police Core Grant, formula funding, and various specific grants (counter-terrorism, serious violence, uplift). Supplemented by local council tax police precept.
Police Core Grant and Formula Funding.b: 56.5 million people in England and Wales served by 43 police forces (~150,000 officers)

Scottish Government Block Grant.d: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Scottish Government, determined by the Barnett Formula. Funds devolved services including NHS Scotland, Scottish education, justice, transport, and local government. Adjusted for tax devolution under the Scotland Act 2016 fiscal framework.

Welsh Government Block Grant.d: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Welsh Government, determined by the Barnett Formula with a needs-based floor introduced in 2015. Funds devolved services including NHS Wales, Welsh education, local government, housing, and transport within Wales.

Northern Ireland Block Grant.d: Block grant from HM Treasury to the Northern Ireland Executive, determined by the Barnett Formula. Funds devolved services including Health and Social Care NI, education, policing (PSNI), justice, and infrastructure. Northern Ireland has the widest devolution settlement in the UK.

MOD Military Personnel Costs.d: Pay, allowances, pensions contributions, and associated personnel costs for UK regular and reserve Armed Forces across the Royal Navy, British Army, and Royal Air Force. Includes military salaries, X-Factor supplement, operational allowances, and employer pension contributions to AFPS.
MOD Military Personnel Costs.b: Approximately 145,000 regular Armed Forces personnel plus 27,000 volunteer reserves

MOD Equipment and Support (Defence Procu.d: Capital and resource expenditure on military equipment procurement, support, and maintenance managed through Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S). Covers major programmes including Dreadnought-class submarines, Type 26 frigates, F-35B Lightning, Ajax AFVs, and Tempest/GCAP future combat air.

Network Rail Government Grant.d: Government grant to Network Rail (reclassified as a public body in 2014) to fund operations, maintenance, and renewals of the railway infrastructure in England and Wales â€” including 20,000 miles of track, 30,000 bridges, and 2,500 stations.
Network Rail Government Grant.b: Approximately 1.7 billion passenger journeys per year on the GB rail network

High Speed 2 (HS2) Construction Programm.d: Capital funding for the construction of the UK’s high-speed railway. Phase 1 runs from London Euston to Birmingham Curzon Street (scheduled to open 2029-33). The Phase 2 extensions to Manchester and Leeds were cancelled in October 2023, with funding redirected to Network North.
High Speed 2 (HS2) Construction Programm.b: Not applicable until operational (estimated 300,000+ daily passengers at full operation)

Revenue Support Grant (RSG) and Local Go.d: Un-ring-fenced central government revenue grant to local authorities in England to support core services. Part of the wider Local Government Finance Settlement which also includes redistributed business rates, specific grants, and the New Homes Bonus.
Revenue Support Grant (RSG) and Local Go.b: 317 local authorities in England serving approximately 56.5 million residents

Affordable Homes Programme (AHP).d: Capital grant programme delivered through Homes England and the Greater London Authority to fund the construction of new affordable housing in England â€” including affordable rent, social rent, shared ownership, and rent-to-buy homes.
Affordable Homes Programme (AHP).b: Target of approximately 180,000 new affordable homes over the 2021-26 programme

NHS Capital Investment (DHSC Capital DEL.d: Capital expenditure on NHS infrastructure in England including new hospital construction (New Hospital Programme), medical equipment, digital and IT (including NHS App, electronic patient records), estates maintenance and backlog reduction, and ambulance fleet.
NHS Capital Investment (DHSC Capital DEL.b: 56.5 million NHS users in England across ~1,200 NHS sites

UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, former.d: Executive agency responsible for protecting the nation from health threats including infectious disease surveillance, outbreak response, immunisation programmes, chemical and radiation hazards. Replaced Public Health England (PHE, est. 2013) following the COVID-19 pandemic review.
UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, former.b: 67 million UK population (health protection is UK-wide for some functions)

Legal Aid (Civil and Criminal).d: Publicly funded legal advice and representation for individuals who cannot afford lawyers, covering criminal defence (duty solicitors, Crown Court advocacy) and civil matters (family, housing, immigration, mental health, debt). Administered by the Legal Aid Agency.
Legal Aid (Civil and Criminal).b: Approximately 1.5 million legal aid acts of assistance per year

HM Prison Service (HM Prison and Probati.d: Operational costs of running 117 prisons in England and Wales, including staff pay, maintenance, prisoner healthcare (commissioned via NHS England), education, food, and security. Managed by HM Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS), an executive agency of MOJ.

Probation Service.d: Supervision and rehabilitation of offenders in the community, including managing community sentences, licence supervision of released prisoners, pre-sentence reports, approved premises (hostels), and electronic monitoring. Returned to full public ownership in 2021.
Probation Service.b: Approximately 250,000 offenders under probation supervision at any time

Border Force.d: Law enforcement command within the Home Office responsible for immigration and customs controls at UK ports and airports, including passport checks, customs enforcement, detection of prohibited goods, and small boat interceptions in the English Channel.
Border Force.b: Not directly applicable (processes ~250 million passenger arrivals per year at UK borders)

Counter-Terrorism Policing and CONTEST P.d: Funding for the UK’s counter-terrorism strategy (CONTEST) comprising four strands: Prevent (deradicalisation), Pursue (investigation and disruption), Protect (infrastructure security), and Prepare (emergency response). Includes CT policing grant to forces, MI5 interface, and Channel programme.

Official Development Assistance (ODA) â€.d: UK bilateral and multilateral overseas development assistance, funding programmes in global health, education, humanitarian response, climate finance, governance, and economic development. Includes contributions to multilateral organisations (World Bank IDA, Global Fund, Gavi, UN agencies).
Official Development Assistance (ODA) â€.b: Hundreds of millions of people across partner countries (UK bilateral ODA reaches 100+ countries)

BBC World Service Grant-in-Aid.d: Government grant to the BBC World Service for international broadcasting in over 40 languages, providing independent news and analysis to global audiences. FCDO funding supplements BBC licence fee funding (which became the primary funder from 2014).
BBC World Service Grant-in-Aid.b: Estimated weekly global audience of 365 million people across radio, TV, and digital

Environment Agency.d: Executive non-departmental public body responsible for environmental regulation in England, including flood risk management, water quality and resources, waste regulation, fisheries, and industrial emissions permitting. Major capital programme in flood defences.
Environment Agency.b: 56.5 million people in England (environmental protection) plus 5.2 million properties in flood risk areas

Natural England.d: Government adviser and delivery body for the natural environment in England, responsible for designating and managing SSSIs, National Nature Reserves, AONBs (now National Landscapes), wildlife licensing, Biodiversity Net Gain, and Environmental Land Management scheme advice.
Natural England.b: Not directly applicable (protects 4,100+ SSSIs covering 8% of England’s land area)

Flood and Coastal Erosion Risk Managemen.d: Capital investment programme to build and maintain flood defences, coastal erosion protection, and surface water management infrastructure across England. Delivered primarily by the Environment Agency with contributions from local levy, partnership funding, and local authorities.
Flood and Coastal Erosion Risk Managemen.b: Target of 336,000 properties better protected from flooding over the 2021-27 programme period

Farming Subsidies (Basic Payment Scheme.d: Direct payments to farmers in England, transitioning from area-based EU Common Agricultural Policy payments (Basic Payment Scheme) to Environmental Land Management (ELM) schemes that pay for environmental outcomes â€” Sustainable Farming Incentive (SFI), Countryside Stewardship (CS), and Landscape Recovery.
Farming Subsidies (Basic Payment Scheme.b: Approximately 85,000 farm businesses in England receiving BPS; 37,000+ with ELM agreements (and growing)

Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA).d: Non-departmental public body responsible for decommissioning and cleaning up the UK’s earliest nuclear sites, including Sellafield, Dounreay, Magnox reactor sites, and associated waste management and spent fuel reprocessing. Manages the UK’s nuclear legacy over a 100+ year programme.
Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA).b: Not directly applicable (environmental cleanup and safety; ~17,000 NDA group employees)

UK Research and Innovation (UKRI).d: Umbrella body for the UK’s seven research councils (EPSRC, BBSRC, MRC, NERC, AHRC, ESRC, STFC), Innovate UK, and Research England. Funds university research grants, fellowships, research infrastructure, innovation partnerships, and the UK’s share of Horizon Europe association.
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI).b: Approximately 90,000 researchers funded; 160+ UK universities and research institutes

Student Loans Company (SLC) Operations a.d: Government-owned company that administers student finance in the UK â€” processing applications, disbursing tuition fee loans and maintenance loans to higher education students in England (and administering devolved schemes for Wales, NI, Scotland). Also handles income-contingent loan repayment collection via HMRC PAYE.
Student Loans Company (SLC) Operations a.b: Approximately 1.8 million students receiving loans annually; total loan book ~Â£236 billion (2024)

Early Years Entitlements (Free Childcare.d: Funded early education and childcare entitlements for children in England: universal 15 hours/week for all 3-4 year olds; extended 30 hours for working parents of 3-4 year olds; 15 hours for disadvantaged 2 year olds; and new entitlements expanding from April 2024 to working parents of 9 months+.
Early Years Entitlements (Free Childcare.b: Approximately 1.7 million children taking up funded places (rising significantly with expansion to under-2s)

Special Educational Needs and Disabiliti.d: Funding for children and young people aged 0-25 with special educational needs and disabilities, covering Education Health and Care Plans (EHCPs), specialist provision in special schools and alternative provision, SEN support in mainstream schools, and post-16 high needs places.
Special Educational Needs and Disabiliti.b: Approximately 1.6 million children with SEN (including 576,000 with EHCPs as of January 2024)

16-19 Education Funding (Post-16 Revenue.d: Revenue funding for post-16 education in England, covering sixth form colleges, school sixth forms, further education (FE) colleges, and independent training providers for students aged 16-19 (and 19-24 with EHCPs). Allocated via the 16-19 national funding formula based on student numbers and programme hours.
16-19 Education Funding (Post-16 Revenue.b: Approximately 1.6 million 16-19 year olds in funded education and training

National Highways (Strategic Road Networ.d: Government-owned company (formerly Highways England, originally Highways Agency) responsible for operating, maintaining, and improving England’s strategic road network â€” approximately 4,500 miles of motorways and major A-roads carrying one-third of all traffic.
National Highways (Strategic Road Networ.b: Approximately 4 million vehicle journeys per day on the strategic road network

Bus Subsidies and Funding (BSIP and BSOG.d: Central government support for bus services in England, comprising Bus Service Operators Grant (BSOG â€” fuel duty rebate), Bus Service Improvement Plans (BSIP) funding, the national Â£2 single fare cap, and reimbursement to operators for statutory concessionary travel (free bus passes for over-66s and disabled people).
Bus Subsidies and Funding (BSIP and BSOG.b: Approximately 3.4 billion bus passenger journeys per year in England; 10 million concessionary pass holders

Arts Council England.d: National development agency for creativity and culture, distributing government and National Lottery funding to arts organisations, museums and libraries across England. Invests in a national portfolio of regularly funded organisations alongside project grants.
Arts Council England.b: Arts organisations, artists, museums, libraries, audiences, communities

British Council.d: UK’s international organisation for cultural relations and educational opportunities, operating in 100+ countries. Delivers English language teaching, educational exchanges, arts and culture programmes, exams administration (IELTS partner), and supports UK soft power objectives.
British Council.b: Approximately 75 million people engaged directly across 100+ countries annually

UK Export Finance (UKEF).d: UK’s official export credit agency providing government-backed loans, guarantees, and insurance to support British exports and overseas investment. Products include buyer credit, direct lending, export insurance, and bond support schemes to help UK exporters win contracts in challenging markets.
UK Export Finance (UKEF).b: Approximately 600 exporters supported per year; maximum portfolio exposure of Â£67 billion

Companies House.d: Executive agency that incorporates and dissolves companies, registers company information and makes it publicly available on its online register. Holds records on over 5 million companies in the UK, with expanded verification powers under the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023.
Companies House.b: Businesses, investors, lenders, law enforcement, the public

Ordnance Survey.d: National mapping agency for Great Britain, providing authoritative geospatial data and mapping products. Operates as a government-owned company and trading fund, generating revenue from data licensing and consumer products.
Ordnance Survey.b: Government agencies, businesses, emergency services, consumers, developers

Met Office.d: UK’s national weather and climate service, providing weather forecasts, severe weather warnings and climate science research. Operates as an executive agency and trading fund of the Ministry of Defence, running some of the world’s most powerful supercomputers for weather modelling.
Met Office.b: General public, emergency services, aviation, defence, agriculture, energy sector

Crown Prosecution Service (CPS).d: Principal public prosecution service for England and Wales, responsible for reviewing and prosecuting criminal cases investigated by the police and other agencies. Applies the Code for Crown Prosecutors (evidential and public interest tests) to decide whether to charge and prosecute.
Crown Prosecution Service (CPS).b: Not directly applicable (criminal justice function serving 56.5 million people in England and Wales; handles ~400,000 defendants per year)

Jobcentre Plus / DWP Operations.d: Executive agency of the Department for Work and Pensions that administers working-age welfare benefits and provides employment support services. Formed by merging the Employment Service and the Benefits Agency to create a single point of contact for benefit claimants and job seekers.
Jobcentre Plus / DWP Operations.b: Working-age adults seeking employment, benefit claimants (JSA, UC), employers recruiting staff

Child Maintenance Group / Child Maintena.d: Replaced the Child Support Agency to arrange and enforce child maintenance payments between separated parents. Operates statutory calculation and collection services where family-based arrangements are not possible.
Child Maintenance Group / Child Maintena.b: Children of separated parents, receiving parents (typically resident parents)

Disability Living Allowance (DLA) — Lega.d: Non-means-tested benefit for people with disabilities who need help with personal care or mobility, introduced by the Disability Living Allowance and Disability Working Allowance Act 1991. Now closed to new working-age claimants (replaced by Personal Independence Payment from 2013) but still paid to existing claimants and children under 16.
Disability Living Allowance (DLA) — Lega.b: Disabled individuals (legacy claimants), disabled children under 16

Bereavement Benefits (DWP).d: Bereavement Support Payment replaced the previous Bereavement Allowance, Widowed Parent’s Allowance, and Bereavement Payment for deaths from April 2017 onwards. Provides a lump sum and up to 18 monthly instalments to surviving spouses or civil partners below State Pension age.
Bereavement Benefits (DWP).b: Surviving spouses and civil partners of deceased persons who paid National Insurance contributions

Industrial Injuries Benefits.d: Scheme providing Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit (IIDB) and related supplements to employees who are disabled as a result of an accident at work or a prescribed industrial disease. Established under the National Insurance (Industrial Injuries) Act 1946 as part of the post-war welfare state.
Industrial Injuries Benefits.b: Workers disabled by workplace accidents or prescribed occupational diseases

Winter Fuel Payment.d: Annual tax-free payment to help older people pay for heating during winter, introduced by the incoming Labour government. Typically paid automatically to eligible recipients in November or December each year.
Winter Fuel Payment.b: Individuals who have reached State Pension age (eligibility tightened from winter 2024/25 to Pension Credit recipients only)

Cold Weather Payment.d: Automatic payment of £25 for each seven-day period of very cold weather (average temperature recorded or forecast to be 0°C or below) in a claimant’s area. Linked to specific qualifying benefits including Pension Credit, Income Support, and income-based JSA/ESA.
Cold Weather Payment.b: Recipients of qualifying means-tested benefits during periods of severe cold weather

Budgeting Loans / Budgeting Advances.d: Interest-free loans from the Social Fund (Budgeting Loans for legacy benefits) or Budgeting Advances (for Universal Credit claimants) to help with intermittent expenses such as furniture, clothing, or moving costs. Repaid through deductions from ongoing benefit payments.
Budgeting Loans / Budgeting Advances.b: Benefit recipients on low incomes facing one-off or intermittent costs

Carer’s Allowance.d: Benefit for people who spend at least 35 hours a week caring for someone receiving a qualifying disability benefit, originally introduced as Invalid Care Allowance. It is the principal income-replacement benefit for unpaid carers and is one of the lowest-value benefits in the UK system.
Carer’s Allowance.b: Unpaid carers aged 16 or over providing substantial regular care to a disabled person

Statutory Sick Pay (SSP).d: Minimum level of sick pay that employers are legally required to pay to eligible employees who are too ill to work, introduced by the Social Security and Housing Benefits Act 1982. Paid for up to 28 weeks in a period of incapacity for work.
Statutory Sick Pay (SSP).b: Employees who are unable to work due to illness and meet minimum earnings and notification requirements

Statutory Maternity Pay / Statutory Pate.d: Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP, introduced 1987) provides up to 39 weeks of pay to qualifying employed mothers; Statutory Paternity Pay (SPP, introduced 2003) provides up to two weeks for fathers or partners. Paid by employers who can reclaim most or all of the cost from HMRC.
Statutory Maternity Pay / Statutory Pate.b: Employed mothers (SMP) and fathers/partners (SPP) of newborn or newly adopted children

Sure Start / Children’s Centres.d: Government programme launched to give every child the best possible start in life by bringing together early education, childcare, health, and family support services in disadvantaged areas. Sure Start Children’s Centres were rolled out nationally from 2004 but many have since closed or been repurposed due to local authority funding reductions.
Sure Start / Children’s Centres.b: Young children (0–5 years) and their families, particularly in disadvantaged communities

Free School Meals Funding.d: Government funding enabling state-funded schools to provide free meals to pupils from low-income families, originating from the Education (Provision of Meals) Act 1906. Currently covers all infant pupils (Reception to Year 2) universally and older pupils whose families receive qualifying benefits.
Free School Meals Funding.b: School-age children from low-income families; all infant pupils in state schools (universal infant FSM since 2014)

Pupil Premium.d: Additional funding allocated to state-funded schools in England for each pupil who is or has been eligible for free school meals, is looked after, or has a parent in the armed forces. Schools have autonomy in how they spend the premium but must demonstrate impact on closing attainment gaps.
Pupil Premium.b: Disadvantaged pupils, looked-after children, and children from service families in state-funded schools

Academy Trust Funding.d: Direct funding from the Education and Skills Funding Agency (ESFA) to academy trusts, which are independent state-funded schools operating outside local authority control under a funding agreement with the Secretary of State. Academies were first established under the Learning and Skills Act 2000 and expanded massively from 2010 under the Academies Act.
Academy Trust Funding.b: Pupils in academy schools across England, multi-academy trusts

Further Education Funding (16–19).d: Funding administered by the Education and Skills Funding Agency for the education and training of 16- to 19-year-olds (and those up to 25 with an Education, Health and Care Plan) in school sixth forms, sixth-form colleges, and further education colleges. Allocated through a national funding formula based on student numbers, programme cost weighting, and disadvantage factors.
Further Education Funding (16–19).b: Young people aged 16–19 in post-compulsory education and training in England

Apprenticeship Funding (ESFA).d: Funding for apprenticeship training in England delivered through the apprenticeship levy (paid by employers with pay bills over £3 million) and co-investment from government for smaller employers. Managed by the Education and Skills Funding Agency through digital apprenticeship service accounts.
Apprenticeship Funding (ESFA).b: Apprentices of all ages, employers offering apprenticeships, approved training providers

Office for Students (OfS).d: Independent regulator of higher education in England, established by the Higher Education and Research Act 2017 to replace the Higher Education Funding Council for England (HEFCE). Regulates university registration, monitors quality and financial sustainability, and administers teaching and access funding.
Office for Students (OfS).b: Higher education students in England, registered higher education providers

UK Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA).d: Government research organisation responsible for the development of fusion energy as a safe, sustainable, and low-carbon energy source, established by the Atomic Energy Authority Act 1954. Operates the MAST Upgrade and JET (now decommissioning) fusion experiments at Culham, Oxfordshire.
UK Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA).b: UK energy sector, fusion research community, future energy consumers

Sellafield Ltd / Nuclear Decommissioning.d: The Nuclear Decommissioning Authority (NDA), established under the Energy Act 2004, is responsible for decommissioning and cleaning up the UK’s earliest nuclear sites. Sellafield Ltd is its largest subsidiary, managing the Sellafield site in Cumbria — Europe’s most complex nuclear decommissioning challenge, consuming the majority of the NDA’s multi-billion pound annual budget.
Sellafield Ltd / Nuclear Decommissioning.b: Local communities near nuclear sites, the environment, future generations through hazard reduction

Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S).d: Bespoke trading entity of the Ministry of Defence responsible for procuring and supporting all of the UK Armed Forces’ equipment, from ships and aircraft to ammunition and rations. Headquartered at MOD Abbey Wood in Bristol with an annual through-put of approximately £20 billion.
Defence Equipment & Support (DE&S).b: UK Armed Forces (Royal Navy, British Army, Royal Air Force)

Defence Science and Technology Laborator.d: Executive agency of the Ministry of Defence providing specialist science and technology research to support UK defence and security. Headquartered at Porton Down, Wiltshire, with a secondary site at Fort Halstead; delivers capabilities across CBRN defence, cyber, and weapons systems.
Defence Science and Technology Laborator.b: UK Armed Forces, Ministry of Defence, wider UK national security community

Government Communications Headquarters (.d: UK signals intelligence and information assurance agency, originating as the Government Code and Cypher School in 1919 and renamed GCHQ in 1946. Responsible for providing signals intelligence to government and the Armed Forces, and for protecting government communications, with its public-facing cyber arm the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) established in 2016.
Government Communications Headquarters (.b: UK government, Armed Forces, critical national infrastructure operators, general public (via cyber security)

Secret Intelligence Service (SIS / MI6).d: UK’s foreign intelligence service responsible for collecting secret intelligence overseas in support of the government’s national security, defence, and foreign policy objectives. Placed on a statutory footing by the Intelligence Services Act 1994 and headquartered at Vauxhall Cross, London.
Secret Intelligence Service (SIS / MI6).b: UK government, national security apparatus, allied intelligence partners

Security Service (MI5).d: UK’s domestic counter-intelligence and security agency responsible for protecting the nation against threats including terrorism, espionage, and the proliferation of weapons of mass destruction. Operates under the Security Service Act 1989 and is headquartered at Thames House, London.
Security Service (MI5).b: UK government, general public, critical national infrastructure

National Crime Agency (NCA).d: National law enforcement agency tackling serious and organised crime that crosses regional and international borders, established by the Crime and Courts Act 2013 to replace the Serious Organised Crime Agency (SOCA). Leads the UK response to cyber crime, human trafficking, drug trafficking, and economic crime.
National Crime Agency (NCA).b: General public, UK law enforcement partners, international crime-fighting agencies

Serious Fraud Office (SFO).d: Specialist prosecuting authority tackling the most complex and serious cases of fraud, bribery, and corruption, established by the Criminal Justice Act 1987. Uniquely combines investigation and prosecution powers in a single organisation and can use Section 2 compulsory powers to obtain documents and testimony.
Serious Fraud Office (SFO).b: Victims of serious fraud and corruption, the financial system, general public

Immigration Enforcement.d: Directorate within the Home Office responsible for enforcing immigration law, including removing those with no legal right to remain, disrupting illegal working, and pursuing immigration-related criminality. Formed as a separate command from the former UK Border Agency in 2012.
Immigration Enforcement.b: General public, lawful migrants (by maintaining system integrity), communities affected by illegal immigration

UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI).d: Part of the Home Office responsible for processing applications from people who want to visit, work, study, or settle in the UK, as well as asylum claims and citizenship applications. Handles millions of visa decisions each year through a global network of visa application centres.
UK Visas and Immigration (UKVI).b: Visa applicants, migrants, asylum seekers, UK-based sponsors (employers, universities, families)

HM Passport Office (HMPO).d: Executive agency of the Home Office that issues, renews, and manages UK passports, re-established as a standalone agency in 2014 having previously been part of the Identity and Passport Service. Processes approximately 7 million passport applications per year.
HM Passport Office (HMPO).b: UK citizens and British nationals requiring travel documents

Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVL.d: Executive agency of the Department for Transport that maintains the registration and licensing of drivers and vehicles in Great Britain, headquartered in Swansea. Holds over 50 million driver records and 40 million vehicle records and collects approximately £7 billion per year in vehicle excise duty.
Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVL.b: Drivers, vehicle owners, motor trade, law enforcement (via records access)

Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVS.d: Executive agency of the Department for Transport formed by the merger of the Driving Standards Agency and the Vehicle and Operator Services Agency. Responsible for conducting driving tests, approving driving instructors, testing vehicles (MOTs), and regulating commercial vehicle operators.
Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVS.b: Learner drivers, driving instructors, vehicle owners, commercial vehicle operators, road users (via road safety)

Maritime and Coastguard Agency (MCA).d: Executive agency of the Department for Transport responsible for implementing the UK’s maritime safety policy, co-ordinating search and rescue at sea through HM Coastguard, and preventing coastal pollution. Formed by merging the Coastguard Agency and the Marine Safety Agency.
Maritime and Coastguard Agency (MCA).b: Seafarers, coastal communities, recreational water users, the marine environment

Civil Aviation Authority (CAA).d: UK’s specialist aviation regulator responsible for safety regulation, airspace policy, consumer protection for air travellers, and economic regulation of airports with significant market power. Established by the Civil Aviation Act 1971 and funded primarily by charges on the aviation industry rather than general taxation.
Civil Aviation Authority (CAA).b: Air passengers, airlines, airports, drone operators, general aviation community

Office for National Statistics (ONS).d: UK’s largest independent producer of official statistics and the recognised national statistical institute, formed by merging the Central Statistical Office and the Office of Population Censuses and Surveys. Responsible for the census, national accounts (GDP), labour market statistics, inflation figures, and population estimates.
Office for National Statistics (ONS).b: Government policy-makers, researchers, businesses, media, general public

HM Land Registry.d: Government department and trading fund that registers the ownership of land and property in England and Wales under the Land Registration Act 2002. Holds over 26 million titles and guarantees the title to registered estates and interests in land.
HM Land Registry.b: Property owners, buyers, sellers, solicitors/conveyancers, mortgage lenders

Valuation Office Agency (VOA).d: Executive agency of HMRC that provides valuations and property advice to the government, most notably compiling and maintaining the council tax valuation lists and non-domestic rating lists used to calculate business rates. Became an executive agency in 1991 but traces its origins to the Valuation Office established in 1910.
Valuation Office Agency (VOA).b: Local authorities (for council tax and business rates), taxpayers, government departments requiring property valuations

National Savings and Investments (NS&I).d: State-owned savings bank and executive agency of HM Treasury that offers savings and investment products backed by HM Treasury, tracing its origins to the Post Office Savings Bank of 1861. Raises cost-effective financing for the government through retail savings products including Premium Bonds, Income Bonds, and Direct Saver accounts.
National Savings and Investments (NS&I).b: Individual savers seeking government-backed products, HM Treasury (as a source of government financing)

UK Debt Management Office (DMO).d: Executive agency of HM Treasury responsible for debt and cash management for the UK government, including issuing gilts (government bonds) and Treasury bills to finance the government’s borrowing requirement. Also manages the government’s Exchequer cash position on a daily basis.
UK Debt Management Office (DMO).b: HM Treasury, gilt market investors, financial institutions, UK taxpayers (through minimised financing costs)

Government Actuary’s Department (GAD).d: Non-ministerial department providing actuarial analysis, advice, and assurance to public sector clients across government on pensions, insurance, and social security matters. Advises on the financial sustainability of public service pension schemes, National Insurance Fund projections, and insurance regulation.
Government Actuary’s Department (GAD).b: Government departments, public sector pension schemes, regulators, public sector workers (indirectly)

Crown Estate.d: Independent commercial business established by the Crown Estate Act 1961, managing a £16 billion property portfolio belonging to the monarch in right of the Crown, including central London real estate, the seabed around England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, and rural estates. Net revenue profits are paid to the Treasury (and partly fund the Sovereign Grant) rather than being retained by the monarch.
Crown Estate.b: HM Treasury (revenue profits), offshore wind developers (seabed leasing), tenants, the Sovereign Grant

Forestry Commission / Forestry England.d: Non-ministerial government department responsible for protecting and expanding England’s forests, established by the Forestry Act 1919 in response to timber shortages during World War I. Forestry England (rebranded from Forest Enterprise England in 2019) manages the nation’s 1,500 public forests covering approximately 250,000 hectares.
Forestry Commission / Forestry England.b: General public (recreation and amenity), timber industry, biodiversity and the environment, rural communities

Rural Payments Agency (RPA).d: Executive agency of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) that administers rural payment schemes in England, including the post-Brexit Environmental Land Management schemes (ELMs) that are replacing the EU’s Common Agricultural Policy Basic Payment Scheme. Also responsible for land mapping, trader services, and rural inspections.
Rural Payments Agency (RPA).b: Farmers and land managers in England, rural businesses, the environment (via agri-environment schemes)

Health Education England (now NHS Englan.d: Responsible for workforce planning, education and training of NHS staff across England. Merged into NHS England in 2023 to consolidate workforce development functions.
Health Education England (now NHS Englan.b: NHS workforce, medical trainees, healthcare students, patients (indirectly)

NHS Blood and Transplant.d: Manages the national blood supply for England and organ donation/transplantation services across the UK. Collects approximately 1.5 million blood donations annually and maintains the NHS Organ Donor Register.
NHS Blood and Transplant.b: Patients requiring blood transfusions, organ transplant recipients, donors

NHS Digital (now NHS England Transformat.d: Provided national IT infrastructure, data and digital services for the health and care system, including the NHS Spine and electronic health records. Merged into NHS England in 2023 as part of the Transformation Directorate.
NHS Digital (now NHS England Transformat.b: NHS trusts, GP practices, patients using digital health services, health researchers

Care Quality Commission (CQC).d: Independent regulator of health and social care services in England, inspecting hospitals, care homes, GP surgeries and dental practices. Rates providers and has powers to enforce improvements or close services.
Care Quality Commission (CQC).b: Patients, care home residents, service users, the general public

National Institute for Health and Care E.d: Produces evidence-based guidance on clinical practice, public health and health technologies for the NHS. Appraises new medicines and treatments to determine cost-effectiveness and recommends whether the NHS should fund them.
National Institute for Health and Care E.b: NHS clinicians, patients, commissioners, pharmaceutical companies (through appraisal process)

Medicines and Healthcare products Regula.d: Regulates medicines, medical devices and blood components for transfusion in the UK, ensuring they meet safety, quality and efficacy standards. Responsible for granting marketing authorisations and monitoring adverse reactions through the Yellow Card scheme.
Medicines and Healthcare products Regula.b: Patients, pharmaceutical and medical device companies, healthcare professionals

Food Standards Agency.d: Independent government department responsible for food safety and food hygiene across England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Oversees food labelling, standards enforcement and the national food hygiene rating scheme.

Animal and Plant Health Agency.d: Executive agency of Defra responsible for safeguarding animal and plant health, preventing the spread of animal diseases such as avian influenza and bovine TB. Carries out surveillance, diagnostic testing and scientific research.
Animal and Plant Health Agency.b: Farmers, livestock keepers, plant growers, the general public

Veterinary Medicines Directorate.d: Executive agency of Defra that regulates the manufacture, marketing and post-authorisation surveillance of veterinary medicines in the UK. Ensures medicines for animals are safe, effective and manufactured to high quality standards.
Veterinary Medicines Directorate.b: Animal owners, veterinary professionals, farmers, pharmaceutical companies

Health and Safety Executive (HSE).d: National regulator for workplace health and safety in Great Britain, responsible for enforcing health and safety legislation and reducing work-related death, injury and ill health. Inspects workplaces, investigates incidents and provides guidance to employers.

Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration S.d: Provides free and impartial advice to employers and employees on workplace relations and employment law. Offers conciliation services for employment disputes, including mandatory early conciliation before tribunal claims.

Employment Tribunals.d: Judicial body that hears claims relating to employment law, including unfair dismissal, discrimination, equal pay and redundancy disputes. Operates as part of HM Courts and Tribunals Service across England, Wales and Scotland.
Employment Tribunals.b: Employees, employers, claimants and respondents in employment disputes

Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority.d: Duplicate of entry 106. Regulates gangmasters supplying workers in agriculture, horticulture and shellfish sectors, and investigates labour exploitation and modern slavery. Uses criminal enforcement powers to prosecute serious labour abuses.
Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority.b: Vulnerable and exploited workers, legitimate labour providers

Low Pay Commission.d: Independent body that advises the government on the National Minimum Wage and National Living Wage rates. Comprises employer, worker and independent representatives who analyse economic evidence to recommend pay floor levels.

Equality and Human Rights Commission.d: National equality body for England, Scotland and Wales, responsible for enforcing equality legislation and protecting human rights. Monitors and promotes equality across the protected characteristics defined in the Equality Act 2010.
Equality and Human Rights Commission.b: Individuals facing discrimination, marginalised groups, employers, public bodies

Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO).d: Independent authority that upholds information rights in the public interest, enforcing the UK GDPR, Data Protection Act 2018 and Freedom of Information Act 2000. Has powers to issue fines, conduct audits and investigate data breaches.
Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO).b: Individuals (data subjects), organisations processing personal data, FOI requesters

Charity Commission.d: Registrar and regulator of charities in England and Wales, responsible for ensuring charities operate for the public benefit and in accordance with charity law. Maintains the public register of charities and investigates mismanagement or misconduct.
Charity Commission.b: Charity trustees, donors, beneficiaries of charitable organisations, the public

Electoral Commission.d: Independent body that oversees elections and regulates political finance in the UK, ensuring elections are well-run and compliant with the law. Registers political parties, monitors campaign spending and sets standards for electoral administration.
Electoral Commission.b: Voters, political parties, candidates, electoral administrators

Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsm.d: Investigates complaints about UK government departments, agencies and NHS bodies in England that have not been resolved through their own complaints procedures. Makes recommendations for redress including apologies, explanations and financial compensation.
Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsm.b: Citizens with unresolved complaints against government or NHS services

National Audit Office (NAO).d: Independent parliamentary body that audits central government departments, agencies and public bodies, reporting to the House of Commons on the economy, efficiency and effectiveness of public spending. Headed by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
National Audit Office (NAO).b: Parliament, taxpayers, government departments (through improvement recommendations)

Government Legal Department.d: Ministerial department providing legal advice and litigation services to most government departments and agencies across England and Wales. Successor to the Treasury Solicitor’s Department, it is one of the largest legal organisations in the country.

Treasury Solicitor.d: Historic office now part of the Government Legal Department, the Treasury Solicitor acts as head of the GLD and provides legal representation for the Crown. Also administers bona vacantia (ownerless property) in England and Wales on behalf of the Crown.
Treasury Solicitor.b: The Crown, government departments, claimants on bona vacantia estates

Crown Commercial Service.d: Executive agency of the Cabinet Office that manages commercial agreements and procurement frameworks on behalf of the public sector, achieving savings through aggregated buying power. Manages over GBP 30 billion of public procurement annually.
Crown Commercial Service.b: Government departments, NHS trusts, local authorities, public sector suppliers

Government Property Agency.d: Executive agency of the Cabinet Office responsible for managing the government’s office estate across England, consolidating property holdings and creating modern shared workplaces. Aims to reduce the government estate footprint and save taxpayer money.

Government Digital Service (GDS).d: Part of the Cabinet Office responsible for the digital transformation of government, maintaining GOV.UK and setting digital standards across the public sector. Develops platforms including GOV.UK Notify, GOV.UK Pay and the GOV.UK Design System.
Government Digital Service (GDS).b: Citizens accessing government services online, government departments, civil servants

Central Digital and Data Office.d: Cabinet Office unit responsible for the government’s digital, data and technology strategy, setting cross-government standards and overseeing major digital programmes. Leads on data sharing, digital identity and the government’s technology spending controls.
Central Digital and Data Office.b: Government departments, digital professionals in government, citizens

UK Statistics Authority.d: Independent body that oversees the Office for National Statistics and monitors the production and use of official statistics across the UK. Has statutory authority to assess official statistics against the Code of Practice for Statistics.
UK Statistics Authority.b: Policymakers, researchers, the public, ONS staff

Boundary Commission for England.d: Independent body that reviews and recommends changes to parliamentary constituency boundaries in England, ensuring roughly equal voter numbers per constituency. Conducts periodic reviews as required under the Parliamentary Constituencies Act.

Office of the Public Guardian.d: Executive agency of the Ministry of Justice that registers lasting powers of attorney and deputyship orders, protecting people who lack mental capacity. Supervises deputies appointed by the Court of Protection and investigates concerns about attorneys or deputies.
Office of the Public Guardian.b: Vulnerable adults lacking mental capacity, their families, attorneys, deputies

Official Receiver / Insolvency Service.d: Executive agency of the Department for Business and Trade that administers compulsory company liquidations and personal bankruptcies, and investigates the conduct of bankrupt individuals and company directors. Maintains public confidence in the insolvency regime.
Official Receiver / Insolvency Service.b: Creditors, bankrupt individuals, employees of insolvent companies, the public

Intellectual Property Office.d: Executive agency of the Department for Business and Trade responsible for granting patents, registering trademarks and designs, and shaping UK intellectual property policy. Operates on a trading fund basis, largely self-financing through fees.
Intellectual Property Office.b: Inventors, businesses, trademark holders, creative industries

UK Space Agency.d: Executive agency of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology responsible for UK civil space policy, managing the UK’s contribution to the European Space Agency and supporting the domestic space sector. Oversees satellite launch regulation and space sustainability.
UK Space Agency.b: Space industry companies, researchers, satellite operators, the public

UK Hydrographic Office.d: Produces nautical charts and hydrographic data for mariners worldwide under the ADMIRALTY brand, supporting safe navigation at sea. Operates as a trading fund of the Ministry of Defence, generating commercial revenue from data products.
UK Hydrographic Office.b: Mariners, shipping companies, Royal Navy, port authorities, offshore energy sector

Defence Infrastructure Organisation.d: Part of the Ministry of Defence responsible for building, maintaining and managing the defence estate across the UK, including military bases, training areas and housing. Manages approximately 1.8% of the UK land mass.
Defence Infrastructure Organisation.b: Armed forces personnel, military families, MoD, defence contractors

Armed Forces Covenant Fund Trust.d: Independent charity that distributes funding from the Armed Forces Covenant Fund to programmes supporting the armed forces community across the UK. Awards grants for projects addressing veterans’ needs including mental health, housing and integration.
Armed Forces Covenant Fund Trust.b: Veterans, serving personnel, military families, armed forces charities

War Pensions / Armed Forces Compensation.d: Government schemes providing financial support to armed forces personnel and veterans injured or bereaved as a result of service. The War Pensions Scheme covers service before 6 April 2005, while the Armed Forces Compensation Scheme covers service from that date onwards.
War Pensions / Armed Forces Compensation.b: Injured veterans, bereaved military families, serving personnel with service-related conditions

Commonwealth War Graves Commission.d: Maintains the graves and memorials of 1.7 million Commonwealth servicemen and women who died in the First and Second World Wars, at 23,000 locations in over 150 countries. Funded jointly by six member governments with the UK contributing the largest share.
Commonwealth War Graves Commission.b: Families of the war dead, veterans’ organisations, the public, historians

British Transport Police.d: National police force responsible for policing railways, light rail systems and the London Underground across England, Wales and Scotland. Funded primarily by the rail industry through charges to train operating companies and Network Rail.
British Transport Police.b: Rail passengers, rail staff, train operating companies, the public

College of Policing.d: Professional body for policing in England and Wales, setting standards, developing training curricula and supporting evidence-based policing practice. Manages police officer recruitment assessments and the national Police National Database.
College of Policing.b: Police officers, police forces, the public

Independent Office for Police Conduct (I.d: Oversees the police complaints system in England and Wales and independently investigates the most serious matters involving police officers. Successor to the Independent Police Complaints Commission, with enhanced powers to initiate investigations without referral.
Independent Office for Police Conduct (I.b: Complainants against police, police officers, the public

Fire and Rescue Service funding.d: Central government grant funding to fire and rescue authorities in England, supplementing council tax income to provide fire prevention, firefighting and rescue services. Distributed via the local government finance settlement alongside other Home Office grants.
Fire and Rescue Service funding.b: Local communities, fire and rescue authorities, firefighters

Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS).d: Helps employers make safer recruitment decisions by processing criminal record checks and maintaining the barred lists of individuals unsuitable to work with children or vulnerable adults. Formed from the merger of the Criminal Records Bureau and the Independent Safeguarding Authority.
Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS).b: Employers, children, vulnerable adults, job applicants

UK Anti-Doping Agency.d: National organisation responsible for protecting clean sport by implementing the UK’s anti-doping policy in compliance with the World Anti-Doping Code. Conducts testing, education programmes and investigations into doping violations.
UK Anti-Doping Agency.b: Clean athletes, sports governing bodies, the sporting public

Sport England.d: Arms-length body of DCMS that invests National Lottery and government funding into grassroots and community sport and physical activity in England. Aims to increase participation and reduce inequalities in access to sport.
Sport England.b: Community sports clubs, local authorities, participants in sport and physical activity

UK Sport.d: Invests National Lottery and government funding in Olympic and Paralympic sports to support elite athletes and deliver medal success at major international competitions. Also supports bids to host major sporting events in the UK.
UK Sport.b: Elite athletes, national governing bodies of sport, Olympic/Paralympic programmes

British Film Institute (BFI).d: Promotes and preserves film culture in the UK, distributing National Lottery funding for film production, distribution and education. Maintains the BFI National Archive, one of the largest film archives in the world, and operates BFI Southbank and BFI IMAX.
British Film Institute (BFI).b: Filmmakers, production companies, cinema audiences, film students, cultural heritage

English Heritage / Historic England.d: Historic England (the public body, established 2015) advises government on the historic environment, manages the heritage list and provides grants for conservation. English Heritage (the charity, separated in 2015) manages over 400 historic properties and sites.
English Heritage / Historic England.b: Heritage site visitors, property owners, local authorities, conservation professionals

British Museum.d: National museum housing a permanent collection of approximately 8 million objects spanning world history, art and culture, with free admission. One of the most visited museums globally, funded primarily by DCMS grant-in-aid alongside self-generated income.
British Museum.b: Museum visitors, researchers, students, the general public

Natural History Museum.d: National museum and leading scientific research institution with a collection of over 80 million specimens spanning 4.5 billion years of life and Earth history. Conducts globally significant research in biodiversity, planetary science and evolution.
Natural History Museum.b: Museum visitors, scientists, students, conservation researchers

Victoria and Albert Museum.d: World’s leading museum of art, design and performance, housing a permanent collection spanning 5,000 years of human creativity. Includes collections of fashion, textiles, ceramics, furniture, photography and digital design.
Victoria and Albert Museum.b: Museum visitors, designers, artists, students, creative industries

Tate Galleries.d: National institution comprising Tate Modern, Tate Britain, Tate Liverpool and Tate St Ives, housing the national collection of British art and international modern and contemporary art. Tate Modern is one of the most visited modern art museums in the world.
Tate Galleries.b: Gallery visitors, artists, art students, researchers, the public

National Gallery.d: Houses the national collection of Western European paintings from the 13th to 19th centuries, with over 2,300 works including masterpieces by Van Gogh, Turner and Leonardo. Offers free admission and is situated in Trafalgar Square, London.
National Gallery.b: Gallery visitors, art students, researchers, the general public

British Library.d: National library of the United Kingdom and one of the largest research libraries in the world, holding over 170 million items including books, manuscripts, maps and sound recordings. Receives a copy of every publication produced in the UK and Ireland through legal deposit.
British Library.b: Researchers, students, businesses, the general public

National Lottery Community Fund (formerl.d: Largest funder of community activity in the UK, distributing approximately GBP 600 million of National Lottery funding annually to community and voluntary organisations. Supports projects focused on community development, wellbeing and reducing inequalities.
National Lottery Community Fund (formerl.b: Community groups, voluntary organisations, social enterprises, local communities

Homes England.d: Government’s housing accelerator, providing funding, land and expertise to support housebuilding and regeneration across England. Successor to the Homes and Communities Agency, it manages the Affordable Homes Programme and Help to Build equity loan scheme.
Homes England.b: Homebuyers, housing associations, developers, local authorities, renters

Building Safety Regulator.d: Established within the Health and Safety Executive under the Building Safety Act 2022 to oversee the safety and performance of all buildings, with a particular focus on higher-risk residential buildings over 18 metres. Regulates building control professionals and manages the new gateway system for building work.
Building Safety Regulator.b: Residents of high-rise buildings, leaseholders, building professionals, the public

Planning Inspectorate.d: Executive agency of DLUHC that processes planning and enforcement appeals, examines local plans and handles applications for nationally significant infrastructure projects. Provides an independent, impartial decision-making service on planning matters.
Planning Inspectorate.b: Planning applicants, local authorities, developers, communities, infrastructure promoters

Ofsted.d: Office for Standards in Education, Children’s Services and Skills inspects and regulates services providing education and skills for learners of all ages, and services caring for children and young people. Publishes inspection reports to inform parents and the public about the quality of provision.
Ofsted.b: Parents, pupils, students, childcare users, schools, colleges

Ofcom.d: UK’s communications regulator overseeing broadband, mobile, postal services, broadcasting and online safety. Regulates spectrum allocation, enforces broadcasting standards and holds new duties under the Online Safety Act 2023.
Ofcom.b: Consumers, telecoms companies, broadcasters, internet users

Ofwat.d: Economic regulator of the water and sewerage sector in England and Wales, setting price limits, service standards and investment requirements for water companies through periodic price reviews. Holds companies to account on performance, leakage and environmental impact.
Ofwat.b: Water bill payers, the environment, water companies

Ofgem.d: Office of Gas and Electricity Markets regulates the gas and electricity markets in Great Britain, protecting consumer interests and overseeing the energy price cap. Administers environmental schemes and regulates network companies’ investment plans.
Ofgem.b: Energy consumers, energy suppliers, network operators

Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).d: Regulates the conduct of approximately 50,000 financial services firms and financial markets in the UK, and acts as prudential regulator for smaller firms. Self-funded through levies on regulated firms, not from public expenditure.
Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).b: Financial services consumers, investors, regulated firms, financial markets

Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA).d: Part of the Bank of England responsible for the prudential regulation and supervision of banks, building societies, credit unions, insurers and major investment firms. Promotes the safety and soundness of the firms it regulates and the stability of the UK financial system.
Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA).b: Bank depositors, insurance policyholders, financial system participants

Financial Ombudsman Service.d: Independent body that resolves disputes between consumers and financial services firms, providing a free alternative to the courts. Handles hundreds of thousands of complaints annually covering banking, insurance, investments and payment services.
Financial Ombudsman Service.b: Consumers with financial services complaints, financial firms

Payment Systems Regulator.d: Economic regulator of the UK’s payment systems, promoting competition, innovation and the interests of service users. Subsidiary of the FCA, it oversees systems such as Faster Payments, BACS and card payment networks.
Payment Systems Regulator.b: Consumers, businesses, payment service providers, fintechs

Competition and Markets Authority (CMA).d: UK’s primary competition and consumer protection authority, investigating mergers, enforcing competition law, conducting market studies and protecting consumers from unfair practices. Gained expanded post-Brexit powers to review mergers previously assessed by the European Commission.

Advertising Standards Authority.d: Independent self-regulatory body for the advertising industry in the UK, enforcing the Advertising Codes across broadcast and non-broadcast media. Industry-funded rather than taxpayer-funded, it adjudicates complaints and ensures adverts are legal, decent, honest and truthful.

Office of Rail and Road (ORR).d: Independent regulator of rail and road in Great Britain, monitoring Network Rail’s performance and efficiency, enforcing health and safety on railways, and monitoring Highways England’s management of the strategic road network. Formerly the Office of Rail Regulation.
Office of Rail and Road (ORR).b: Rail passengers, freight operators, road users, Network Rail, train companies

Transport for London (TfL) government gr.d: Central government provides capital and operating grants to Transport for London to support the Tube, buses, Overground, DLR and road network across London. Grant levels have varied significantly, with extraordinary funding deals agreed during and after the COVID-19 pandemic.
Transport for London (TfL) government gr.b: London commuters, bus and Tube users, London businesses, TfL

Crossrail/Elizabeth line residual costs.d: Residual government funding for the Elizabeth line (Crossrail), which opened in stages in 2022 after significant cost overruns from the original GBP 14.8 billion budget. Covers final completion works, defects liability and financing costs from additional borrowing.
Crossrail/Elizabeth line residual costs.b: Elizabeth line passengers, London and South East commuters, the rail network

Northern Powerhouse Rail.d: Strategic transport programme to improve east-west rail connectivity across the North of England, linking major cities including Leeds, Manchester, Sheffield and Liverpool. Scope was significantly scaled back in the 2021 Integrated Rail Plan compared to original proposals.
Northern Powerhouse Rail.b: Northern England commuters, businesses, northern city regions

Great British Railways (transition).d: Proposed public body to integrate rail track and train operations under a single national leadership, replacing the current fragmented franchise system. The Great British Railways Transition Team operates pending legislation, taking over some functions from the former Rail Delivery Group.
Great British Railways (transition).b: Rail passengers, freight operators, train operating companies, railway workers

UK Infrastructure Bank.d: Government-owned policy bank headquartered in Leeds, providing loans and equity finance to support private infrastructure investment in clean energy, transport and digital connectivity. Established with GBP 22 billion of financial capacity to crowd in private capital.
UK Infrastructure Bank.b: Infrastructure developers, clean energy projects, local authorities, private investors

British Business Bank.d: Government-owned economic development bank that supports smaller businesses to access finance through guarantees, loans and venture capital programmes. Manages schemes including Start Up Loans, the Enterprise Finance Guarantee and the Future Fund.
British Business Bank.b: SMEs, start-ups, entrepreneurs, venture capital funds

Innovate UK.d: UK’s national innovation agency, part of UK Research and Innovation, providing grants, loans and support to businesses developing new products, processes and services. Runs programmes such as the Knowledge Transfer Network and Catapult centres.
Innovate UK.b: Innovative businesses, start-ups, universities, research institutions

Research England.d: Council within UK Research and Innovation responsible for distributing block grant research and knowledge exchange funding to English universities, including Quality-Related (QR) research funding based on the Research Excellence Framework. Created as part of the UKRI structure under the Higher Education and Research Act 2017.

Engineering and Physical Sciences Resear.d: Largest of the seven UKRI research councils by budget, funding research and training in engineering and the physical sciences including mathematics, computer science, materials and manufacturing. Supports approximately one-third of all publicly funded research in the UK.
Engineering and Physical Sciences Resear.b: University researchers, PhD students, engineering and physical sciences communities

Medical Research Council (MRC).d: UKRI research council funding biomedical and clinical research to improve human health, with a portfolio spanning molecular science, global health, neuroscience and population studies. Operates research units and institutes including the Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge.
Medical Research Council (MRC).b: Medical researchers, clinicians, patients (through translational research), PhD students

Natural Environment Research Council (NE.d: UKRI research council funding environmental science research across atmospheric, earth, biological, hydrological and marine sciences. Operates the British Antarctic Survey, British Geological Survey and National Oceanography Centre.
Natural Environment Research Council (NE.b: Environmental scientists, ecologists, climate researchers, PhD students

Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Re.d: UKRI research council investing in bioscience research and training spanning food security, industrial biotechnology, agriculture and world-class fundamental bioscience. Funds strategically important research institutes including Rothamsted Research and the Roslin Institute.
Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Re.b: Bioscience researchers, agriculture sector, food industry, PhD students

Science and Technology Facilities Counci.d: UKRI research council that operates and provides access to large-scale scientific facilities including the Diamond Light Source, ISIS Neutron and Muon Source, and major telescopes. Supports particle physics, nuclear physics, astronomy and space science research.
Science and Technology Facilities Counci.b: Physicists, astronomers, facility users, PhD students, international research collaborations

Economic and Social Research Council (ES.d: UKRI research council funding economic and social science research, including major longitudinal studies and data infrastructure. Supports research addressing societal challenges including productivity, inequality, wellbeing and public policy.
Economic and Social Research Council (ES.b: Social scientists, economists, policymakers, PhD students

Arts and Humanities Research Council (AH.d: UKRI research council funding research and postgraduate training in the arts and humanities, from history and languages to design and creative practice. Supports heritage science, cultural understanding and creative economy research.
Arts and Humanities Research Council (AH.b: Arts and humanities researchers, PhD students, creative industries, cultural sector

Alan Turing Institute.d: UK’s national institute for data science and artificial intelligence, headquartered at the British Library. Founded by five universities and backed by EPSRC funding, it conducts research in AI, data science, and their applications to real-world problems.
Alan Turing Institute.b: AI and data science researchers, industry partners, government, PhD students

Francis Crick Institute.d: Europe’s largest biomedical research institute, located in central London, conducting discovery research to understand the fundamental biology underlying health and disease. Funded by the MRC, Cancer Research UK and the Wellcome Trust as a partnership.
Francis Crick Institute.b: Biomedical researchers, postdoctoral scientists, PhD students, patients (long-term)

Joint Nature Conservation Committee.d: Public body that advises the UK government and devolved administrations on nature conservation, providing evidence and scientific advice on UK-wide and international conservation issues. Established under the Environmental Protection Act 1990.
Joint Nature Conservation Committee.b: Government, devolved nature conservation bodies, wildlife, the environment

Marine Management Organisation.d: Executive NDPB of Defra responsible for marine planning, licensing and enforcement in English waters, implementing the UK Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009. Manages marine protected areas, fishing quotas and marine development consents.
Marine Management Organisation.b: Fishing industry, marine developers, coastal communities, marine environment

Canal & River Trust.d: Charity that manages and maintains 2,000 miles of canals and rivers in England and Wales, formerly managed by British Waterways. Receives a declining government grant alongside self-generated income from property, utilities and fundraising.
Canal & River Trust.b: Boaters, towpath users, waterside communities, wildlife, heritage visitors

DEPARTMENT FOR WORK AND PENSIONS (GBP297.d: The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) is the UK government department responsible for welfare, pensions, and child maintenance policy. It administers the state pension, Universal Credit, disability benefits, and employment support programmes.
DEPARTMENT FOR WORK AND PENSIONS (GBP297.b: Pensioners, unemployed individuals, disabled persons, low-income families, and all UK residents eligible for social security benefits.

Department for Work and Pensions (GBP296.d: Core departmental spending line for DWP, covering its direct operational costs and programme expenditure for welfare and pension administration. This is the department-level entity within OSCAR as distinct from the total departmental group which includes arms-length bodies.
Department for Work and Pensions (GBP296.b: Pensioners, benefit claimants, jobseekers, disabled individuals, and families receiving welfare support.

DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH (GBP214.2B).d: The Department of Health (now Department of Health and Social Care) is the UK government department responsible for health and adult social care policy in England. It oversees the NHS, public health, and health-related research funding.
DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH (GBP214.2B).b: All residents of England accessing NHS services, social care users, healthcare workers, and public health beneficiaries.

10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP143.d: COFOG (Classification of Functions of Government) category for state pension expenditure, covering the basic State Pension and new State Pension payments to retirees. This is the single largest welfare spending line in the UK budget.
10.2 Old age; of which: pensions (GBP143.b: Men and women above State Pension age (currently 66) who have made sufficient National Insurance contributions.

DEPARTMENT FOR EDUCATION (GBP140.3B).d: The Department for Education (DfE) is responsible for children’s services and education policy in England, including early years, schools, higher and further education, and apprenticeships. It was re-established in 2010 from the former Department for Children, Schools and Families.
DEPARTMENT FOR EDUCATION (GBP140.3B).b: Children, students, teachers, schools, academies, universities, apprentices, and families in England.

NHS Trusts (GBP135.5B).d: NHS Trusts are public sector bodies established to provide hospital and community health services within the National Health Service. They manage hospitals, ambulance services, mental health services, and community health services across England.
NHS Trusts (GBP135.5B).b: All patients and residents in England accessing hospital, ambulance, mental health, and community health services.

Department for Education (GBP132.9B).d: Core departmental spending line for the DfE, representing direct programme and administrative expenditure on education policy, school funding, and children’s services. Distinguished from the wider departmental group total which includes academies and other bodies.
Department for Education (GBP132.9B).b: Schools, pupils, students, teachers, and educational institutions in England.

MINISTRY OF DEFENCE (GBP71.9B).d: The Ministry of Defence (MoD) is the UK government department responsible for implementing defence policy, managing the Armed Forces, and procuring military equipment. It covers the Royal Navy, British Army, and Royal Air Force.
MINISTRY OF DEFENCE (GBP71.9B).b: UK Armed Forces personnel, veterans, defence industry contractors, and the UK population through national security.

Ministry of Defence (GBP71.4B).d: Core departmental spending line for the MoD, covering military operations, personnel costs, equipment procurement, and estate management. This represents the department-level entity within OSCAR.
Ministry of Defence (GBP71.4B).b: Armed Forces personnel, defence civilians, veterans, and defence contractors.

10.7 Social exclusion n.e.c.; of which:.d: COFOG category covering means-tested benefits and tax credits aimed at reducing social exclusion, including Universal Credit, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Housing Benefit, and legacy tax credits. This captures welfare spending not classified under other social protection subcategories.
10.7 Social exclusion n.e.c.; of which:.b: Low-income families, unemployed persons, housing benefit claimants, and individuals receiving means-tested welfare support.

Northern Ireland Executive (GBP32.9B).d: Core spending entity for the Northern Ireland Executive within OSCAR, covering devolved spending on health, education, infrastructure, agriculture, and other transferred policy areas in Northern Ireland.

4.9 Economic affairs n.e.c..b: Businesses, regional economies, economic development professionals, and communities targeted by growth initiatives

7.B Medical research.d: Government spending on medical and health research through bodies like the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR), Medical Research Council, and NHS R&D funding. Covers clinical trials, biomedical research, and health technology assessment.
7.B Medical research.b: Patients (through improved treatments), medical researchers, pharmaceutical companies, and the healthcare system

10.6 Housing.d: Social protection spending specifically on housing support, primarily Housing Benefit and the housing element of Universal Credit. Covers rent subsidies for low-income tenants in both social and private rented sectors.
10.6 Housing.b: Low-income tenants, private renters, housing association tenants, and people at risk of homelessness

6.6 Housing and community amenities n.e..d: Housing and community spending not elsewhere classified, including community facilities, neighbourhood renewal, urban regeneration programmes, and miscellaneous community amenities not fitting other housing sub-categories.
6.6 Housing and community amenities n.e..b: Local communities, residents of regeneration areas, and community facility users

Statistics Board.d: The UK Statistics Authority (branded as the Statistics Board in OSCAR) is an independent body overseeing the Office for National Statistics. It monitors and reports on the quality of official statistics and promotes good practice in statistical matters.
Statistics Board.b: Government decision makers, researchers, businesses, journalists, and the public relying on accurate official statistics

Department for Culture, Media and Sport.d: DCMS (now Department for Culture, Media, Sport) is responsible for culture, arts, media, sport, tourism, telecoms, gambling, data protection, and the creative industries. It oversees bodies like Sport England, Arts Council, and national museums.
Department for Culture, Media and Sport.b: Cultural and creative industries, sports organisations, media companies, tourists, and the general public
