10.1 Sickness and disability; of which:.b: People with disabilities, those unable to work due to illness, injured workers, and their dependants

10.5 Unemployment; of which: other unemp.b: Unemployed individuals and the unemployment benefits system

Atomic Weapons Establishment (AWE) plc (.b: National defence through nuclear deterrent capability

Office of Rail Regulation.d: Former name of the Office of Rail and Road (ORR). Regulated railway health and safety, network access, and Network Rail’s performance before gaining road monitoring responsibilities in 2015.
Office of Rail Regulation.b: Rail passengers, train operators, Network Rail, and railway workers

6.1 of which: other social housing (GBP0.b: Social housing tenants and housing associations

10.3 Survivors (GBP0.0B).b: Bereaved individuals and survivors of deceased benefit claimants

Driver and Vehicle Testing Agency.d: DVTA is a Northern Ireland executive agency that provides driver testing, vehicle testing (MOT equivalent), and enforcement services for road safety in Northern Ireland. The equivalent of DVSA in England.
Driver and Vehicle Testing Agency.b: Drivers, vehicle owners in Northern Ireland, road safety, and the motoring public

10.7 Social exclusion n.e.c.; of which:.d: Minor spending on personal social services addressing social exclusion, representing small-scale programmes or adjustments in anti-exclusion service provision.

9.4 Tertiary education (GBP0.0B).b: University students and higher education institutions

9.8 Education n.e.c. (GBP0.0B).d: Minor miscellaneous education expenditure not classified under other education categories.

4.5 of which: other transport (GBP0.0B).b: Users of ports, airports, and other transport infrastructure

4.5 of which: railway (GBP0.0B).d: Minor railway expenditure entries, representing specific rail scheme costs or adjustments.
4.5 of which: railway (GBP0.0B).b: Rail passengers and the railway industry

1.2 Foreign economic aid (GBP0.0B entrie.b: Developing nations and international development programmes

2.3 Foreign military aid (GBP0.1B).b: Allied and partner nations’ armed forces, international security, and global stability

3.2 Fire-protection services (GBP0.0B).d: Minor fire protection expenditure at the national level, as fire services are primarily locally funded.

4.7 Other industries (GBP0.0B entries).d: Minor spending on other industries not classified elsewhere.

4.8 R&D economic affairs (GBP0.0B entrie.d: Minor R&D spending for economic affairs, representing specific innovation or research programme costs.

4.9 Economic affairs n.e.c. (GBP0.0B ent.b: The broader economy and economic development

6.5 R&D housing and community amenities.b: Housing researchers and the housing sector

8.1 Recreational and sporting services (.b: Sports participants and recreational facility users

8.2 Cultural services (GBP0.0B entries).b: Cultural institutions, artists, and the public

8.6 Recreation, culture and religion n.e.d: Minor recreation, culture, and religion spending not elsewhere classified.

9.1 of which: under fives (GBP0.2B).b: Children under 5, parents, and early years providers

9.6 Subsidiary services to education (GB.b: School pupils, students, and families accessing education support

6.4 Street lighting (GBP0.0B).b: Pedestrians, road users, and community safety

4.5 of which: railway (GBP0.3B and GBP0..b: Rail passengers, freight operators, and railway communities

S4C.d: S4C (Sianel Pedwar Cymru) is the Welsh-language public-service television channel, established under the Broadcasting Act 1980 and launched on 1 November 1982. It commissions and broadcasts Welsh-language programming across news, drama, sport, children’s content, and entertainment, serving as the principal broadcast platform for the Welsh language.
S4C.b: Welsh-language speakers and learners across Wales and the UK (approximately 900,000 Welsh speakers; wider Welsh cultural community)

DEPARTMENT FOR WORK AND PENSIONS.d: The largest spending department in the UK government, responsible for the State Pension, Universal Credit, disability benefits (PIP, DLA, Attendance Allowance), Housing Benefit, and Employment Support Allowance. Also oversees the Health and Safety Executive and The Pensions Regulator.
DEPARTMENT FOR WORK AND PENSIONS.b: 12.7 million State Pension recipients, 5.5 million Universal Credit claimants, 2.6 million PIP claimants, and millions more receiving other working-age and disability benefits.

DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH.d: Funds the National Health Service (NHS), the world's fifth-largest employer. Covers NHS England hospital and community health services, primary care (GP contracts, prescribing, dental, ophthalmic), specialised commissioning, mental health, public health, social care grants, and capital investment.
DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH.b: The entire population of England through NHS services. NHS treats 1.3 million patients every 24 hours with 1.4 million staff across 340 million GP appointments per year.

DEPARTMENT FOR EDUCATION.d: Funds schools in England through the Dedicated Schools Grant (DSG), 16-19 education, student loans, early years provision, SEND (Special Educational Needs and Disabilities), and academies. England's system is now approximately 80% academised.
DEPARTMENT FOR EDUCATION.b: 8.9 million pupils in state schools, 500,000 teachers, 1.7 million children in funded early years provision, and families across England.

MINISTRY OF DEFENCE.d: Funds the UK's armed forces covering military personnel, equipment procurement and support (including the nuclear deterrent), infrastructure, and operations/NATO commitments. Equipment plan totals GBP 288 billion over 10 years.
MINISTRY OF DEFENCE.b: 148,000 regular forces, 60,000 MOD civilians, and their families. Maintains 2 aircraft carriers, 4 nuclear submarines under the Continuous At Sea Deterrent.

HM TREASURY.d: Central government finance covering interest and management costs on UK national debt (approximately GBP 2.7 trillion, 98% of GDP). Debt interest payments surged after 2022 due to inflation exposure on index-linked gilts. Also covers the core Treasury department.
HM TREASURY.b: All UK taxpayers indirectly, as debt interest consumes a growing share of government revenue. Core Treasury staff of approximately 2,200.

SCOTTISH GOVERNMENT.d: Barnett formula block grant funding devolved services in Scotland: NHS Scotland, education, policing (Police Scotland), transport, local government, and justice. Scotland has had its own parliament since 1999 with substantial devolved powers.
SCOTTISH GOVERNMENT.b: 5.45 million residents of Scotland. Public spending per person in Scotland is approximately 20% higher than the UK average.

HM REVENUE AND CUSTOMS.d: Tax administration, collection, and enforcement. Also administers tax credits and child benefit payments. Collects approximately GBP 820 billion in tax revenue annually across income tax, NICs, VAT, corporation tax, and other duties.
HM REVENUE AND CUSTOMS.b: All UK taxpayers and benefit recipients. 12 million Self Assessment returns processed per year, with 65,000 employees.

HOME OFFICE.d: Covers police funding for 43 forces in England and Wales, immigration and asylum processing, counter-terrorism, Border Force, and fire service support. The asylum backlog peaked at 175,000 cases in 2023.
HOME OFFICE.b: 145,000 police officers in England and Wales, asylum seekers, visa applicants (6 million decisions per year), and the general public through security services.

MINISTRY OF JUSTICE.d: Funds courts and tribunals, the prison and probation service (HM Prison and Probation Service), legal aid (civil and criminal), and the judiciary. England and Wales have approximately 83,000 prison places.
MINISTRY OF JUSTICE.b: All persons involved in legal proceedings, approximately 83,000 prisoners, legal aid recipients, and communities benefiting from probation services.

MINISTRY OF HOUSING, COMMUNITIES AND LOC.d: Funds local government through the Revenue Support Grant and business rates retention, affordable housing programmes, homelessness prevention, building safety, and levelling-up funds. 317 local authorities in England are funded through this department.
MINISTRY OF HOUSING, COMMUNITIES AND LOC.b: Local authorities and their residents. 1.3 million households on social housing waiting lists. Council funding was cut approximately 40% in real terms between 2010 and 2020.

DEPARTMENT FOR TRANSPORT.d: Funds rail infrastructure and subsidies (Network Rail: 20,000 miles of track), strategic roads (National Highways), HS2 construction, bus subsidies, and local transport grants. HS2 revised budget is approximately GBP 66 billion for Phase 1.
DEPARTMENT FOR TRANSPORT.b: All transport users. 32 billion vehicle miles on English roads annually, plus rail passengers, bus users, and communities benefiting from local transport investment.

NORTHERN IRELAND EXECUTIVE.d: Barnett formula block grant and locally raised revenues funding devolved services in Northern Ireland including health, education, policing (PSNI), justice, and agriculture. Northern Ireland has unique cross-border arrangements under the Belfast/Good Friday Agreement.
NORTHERN IRELAND EXECUTIVE.b: 1.9 million residents of Northern Ireland receiving devolved public services.

WELSH ASSEMBLY GOVERNMENT.d: Barnett formula block grant funding devolved services in Wales including NHS Wales, education, transport, local government, and the Welsh language. The Senedd (Welsh Parliament) has had law-making powers since 2011.
WELSH ASSEMBLY GOVERNMENT.b: 3.1 million residents of Wales receiving devolved public services.

SCOTLAND OFFICE AND OFFICE OF THE ADVOCA.d: UK Government department representing Scotland's interests within the UK Government and handling the constitutional relationship between Scotland and the UK. The Office of the Advocate General provides legal advice on Scottish law matters.
SCOTLAND OFFICE AND OFFICE OF THE ADVOCA.b: Scottish residents through representation of Scottish interests in UK Government, and the UK Government through legal advice on Scottish devolution matters.

NORTHERN IRELAND OFFICE.d: UK Government department responsible for Northern Ireland affairs, constitutional matters, and the political process. Oversees the UK Government's relationship with the Northern Ireland Executive and legacy issues.
NORTHERN IRELAND OFFICE.b: Residents of Northern Ireland through UK Government oversight of devolution and legacy programs.

WALES OFFICE.d: UK Government department representing Wales within the UK Government, handling the constitutional relationship between Wales and the UK, and ensuring Welsh interests are considered in UK-wide policy.
WALES OFFICE.b: Welsh residents through representation of Welsh interests in UK Government.

DEPARTMENT FOR ENERGY SECURITY AND NET Z.d: Department responsible for energy policy, energy security, and achieving net zero carbon emissions by 2050. Covers nuclear decommissioning, renewable energy support, energy efficiency, and the UK's energy strategy.
DEPARTMENT FOR ENERGY SECURITY AND NET Z.b: All UK energy consumers, the energy industry, and communities affected by energy infrastructure and the transition to net zero.

CABINET OFFICE.d: Supports the Prime Minister and Cabinet, coordinates government policy, manages the civil service, and oversees government efficiency programs. Includes the Government Digital Service (GDS) and the National Cyber Security Centre.
CABINET OFFICE.b: All government departments through coordination, the civil service through HR and reform programs, and citizens through digital government services.

DEPARTMENT FOR SCIENCE, INNOVATION AND T.d: Funds UK research and innovation through UKRI (UK Research and Innovation), digital infrastructure, AI strategy, telecommunications regulation, and technology R&D. Created in 2023 from parts of the former BEIS.
DEPARTMENT FOR SCIENCE, INNOVATION AND T.b: Researchers, universities, tech businesses, and the broader economy through R&D investment and digital infrastructure.

DEPARTMENT FOR CULTURE, MEDIA AND SPORT.d: Funds arts, culture, sport, tourism, media policy, and gambling/licensing regulation. Includes Arts Council England, Sport England, the British Museum, and other national cultural institutions.
DEPARTMENT FOR CULTURE, MEDIA AND SPORT.b: Cultural institutions and their visitors, athletes, sports participants, the creative industries, and media organizations.

FOREIGN, COMMONWEALTH AND DEVELOPMENT OF.d: Merged department covering diplomatic operations, consular services, development aid (ODA), and the UK's international relationships. Operates 275 posts in 190 countries.
FOREIGN, COMMONWEALTH AND DEVELOPMENT OF.b: UK citizens abroad, partner countries receiving development aid, and the broader population through diplomatic representation and international security.

DEPARTMENT FOR ENVIRONMENT, FOOD AND RUR.d: Post-Brexit farming subsidies (replacing EU CAP with Environmental Land Management), flood defence, the Environment Agency, Natural England, and waste/water regulation. ELM targets 70% of farmland by 2028.
DEPARTMENT FOR ENVIRONMENT, FOOD AND RUR.b: 210,000 farms in England, communities at risk from flooding (5.2 million properties), and the broader population through environmental protection.

DEPARTMENT FOR BUSINESS AND TRADE.d: Supports business growth, trade policy, competition, consumer protection, and post-Brexit trade agreements. Includes Companies House, the Insolvency Service, and UK Export Finance. Created in 2023 from parts of the former BEIS.
DEPARTMENT FOR BUSINESS AND TRADE.b: UK businesses, exporters, consumers, and workers through trade facilitation, regulation, and business support programs.

CABINET OFFICE: CIVIL SUPERANNUATION.d: Civil Service pension scheme payments for retired civil servants, managed under the Principal Civil Service Pension Scheme (now alpha scheme for new entrants). Unfunded defined benefit scheme with liabilities paid from current expenditure.
CABINET OFFICE: CIVIL SUPERANNUATION.b: Retired civil servants and their dependents receiving pension payments from the Principal Civil Service Pension Scheme.

TEACHERS' PENSION SCHEME (ENGLAND AND WA.d: Pension payments for retired teachers in England and Wales under the Teachers' Pension Scheme. An unfunded defined benefit scheme where current contributions from serving teachers and employers fund current pension payments.
TEACHERS' PENSION SCHEME (ENGLAND AND WA.b: Retired teachers and their dependents. Approximately 740,000 active members and growing numbers of pensioners.

ARMED FORCES PENSION AND COMPENSATION SC.d: Pension and compensation payments for former armed forces personnel. Includes the Armed Forces Pension Scheme, the War Pension Scheme, and the Armed Forces Compensation Scheme for injury and illness attributable to service.
ARMED FORCES PENSION AND COMPENSATION SC.b: Veterans and their dependents receiving service-related pensions and compensation for injuries sustained during military service.

CROWN PROSECUTION SERVICE.d: The principal public prosecution service for England and Wales, responsible for reviewing and prosecuting criminal cases investigated by the police and other agencies. Independent of the police and government.
CROWN PROSECUTION SERVICE.b: Victims of crime, defendants, and the public through the administration of criminal justice.

NATIONAL CRIME AGENCY.d: The UK's national law enforcement agency tackling serious and organised crime, including drug trafficking, human trafficking, cybercrime, economic crime, and child exploitation. Works across borders and with international partners.
NATIONAL CRIME AGENCY.b: The UK public through disruption of serious and organised crime networks affecting communities nationwide.

ROYAL MAIL STATUTORY PENSION SCHEME.d: Pension payments under the Royal Mail Statutory Pension Scheme, which was taken over by the government in 2012 as part of Royal Mail's privatisation. An unfunded liability now paid from current government expenditure.
ROYAL MAIL STATUTORY PENSION SCHEME.b: Former Royal Mail employees and their dependents receiving pension payments.

HOUSE OF COMMONS: ADMINISTRATION.d: Administrative costs of the House of Commons including staff, facilities management, IT, security, and support for Members of Parliament in their parliamentary duties.
HOUSE OF COMMONS: ADMINISTRATION.b: Members of Parliament and parliamentary staff who carry out legislative and scrutiny functions.

HOUSE OF LORDS.d: Administrative and operational costs of the House of Lords, the upper chamber of the UK Parliament. Includes staff costs, allowances for peers, and estate maintenance.
HOUSE OF LORDS.b: Members of the House of Lords who participate in the legislative process, and the public through parliamentary scrutiny.

FOREIGN, COMMONWEALTH AND DEVELOPMENT OF.d: Pension payments for former Overseas Civil Service staff, primarily from the colonial era and subsequent diplomatic and development service abroad.
FOREIGN, COMMONWEALTH AND DEVELOPMENT OF.b: Retired former Overseas Civil Service officers and their dependents.

MINISTRY OF JUSTICE: JUDICIAL PENSIONS S.d: Pension payments for retired judges and members of the judiciary under the Judicial Pensions Scheme.

Local Authorities (England).d: Aggregate spending by local authorities in England covering education, social care (adult and children's), police, fire and rescue, highways, housing, public health, environmental services, and cultural services. England has 317 local authorities funded through council tax, business rates retention, and central government grants.
Local Authorities (England).b: All residents of England who receive local government services. 1.3 million households on social housing waiting lists. Local authorities employ over 2 million people.

Education Services.d: Local authority spending on education including maintained schools (non-academy), special educational needs provision, school improvement, early years, and education support services. The majority of school funding now flows directly to academies via the DfE.
Education Services.b: Pupils in maintained schools, children with special educational needs, and families using local authority education services.

Adult Social Care.d: Local authority spending on care and support for adults with physical disabilities, learning disabilities, mental health conditions, and older people requiring residential, nursing, or domiciled care. Councils are the main commissioners and providers of adult social care in England.
Adult Social Care.b: Approximately 1.9 million adults receiving publicly funded social care services, their families and carers, and the adult social care workforce.

Police Services.d: Local authority-related police expenditure covering the 43 police forces in England and Wales, funded through a combination of central government police grant, council tax precepts, and specific grants.
Police Services.b: All residents of England and Wales through policing services. Approximately 145,000 police officers and support staff.

Children's Social Care.d: Local authority spending on safeguarding and supporting vulnerable children, including looked-after children (foster care, residential care), child protection, early help services, and support for care leavers.
Children's Social Care.b: Approximately 83,000 looked-after children, 50,000 children on child protection plans, and families receiving early help and support.

Environmental and Regulatory Services.d: Local authority spending on environmental health, trading standards, licensing, waste collection and disposal, recycling, street cleaning, and regulatory enforcement.
Environmental and Regulatory Services.b: All local residents through waste collection, street cleaning, environmental health inspections, and consumer protection.

Highways and Transport.d: Local authority spending on local road maintenance and construction, street lighting, parking, local bus support, concessionary travel (free bus passes for over-60s and disabled persons), and traffic management.
Highways and Transport.b: All road users and public transport passengers. Concessionary travel benefits approximately 10 million older and disabled persons.

Central Services.d: Local authority spending on central administrative functions including council management, democratic services, legal services, HR, IT, finance, and corporate property.
Central Services.b: All council tax payers and service users through efficient administration of local government functions.

Public Health.d: Local authority public health spending transferred from the NHS in 2013, covering sexual health services, drug and alcohol treatment, health visiting, school nursing, obesity prevention, smoking cessation, and public health advice.
Public Health.b: All local residents through preventive health services, with particular focus on deprived communities and vulnerable groups.

Housing Services.d: Local authority spending on housing services including homelessness prevention and relief, management of council housing stock (Housing Revenue Account), housing advice, and supporting people in temporary accommodation.
Housing Services.b: Households in council housing, homeless persons and those at risk of homelessness, and households in temporary accommodation (over 100,000 households in England).

Fire and Rescue Services.d: Spending on fire and rescue services across England, covering fire prevention, firefighting, road traffic accident response, and community safety. England has 45 fire and rescue authorities.
Fire and Rescue Services.b: All residents through fire protection and emergency response. Approximately 32,000 whole-time and 9,000 retained firefighters.

Cultural and Related Services.d: Local authority spending on libraries, museums, galleries, sports and leisure facilities, parks and open spaces, tourism, and community events.
Cultural and Related Services.b: Local residents using libraries, leisure centres, parks, museums, and other cultural and recreational facilities.

Planning and Development.d: Local authority spending on planning applications, building control, economic development, regeneration, and land use planning. Funded through planning fees and central government grants.
Planning and Development.b: Property developers, businesses, homeowners applying for planning permission, and communities benefiting from regeneration and development control.

Other Services.d: Miscellaneous local authority expenditure not classified under the main service headings, including parish councils, emergency planning, coroners, and other residual spending categories.
Other Services.b: Various local residents and stakeholders benefiting from ancillary local government services.

THE STATISTICS BOARD.d: Funds the UK Statistics Authority, which oversees the Office for National Statistics (ONS) and the regulation of official statistics across government. Responsible for the census, GDP estimates, inflation figures, and labour market statistics.
THE STATISTICS BOARD.b: All users of official statistics including government, businesses, researchers, media, and the public.

INDEPENDENT PARLIAMENTARY STANDARDS AUTH.d: The independent body that sets and administers the pay, pensions, and business costs of Members of Parliament. Created in 2009 following the parliamentary expenses scandal to ensure transparent and accountable MP remuneration.
INDEPENDENT PARLIAMENTARY STANDARDS AUTH.b: 650 Members of Parliament whose pay and expenses are regulated by IPSA.

NATIONAL SAVINGS AND INVESTMENTS.d: A government-backed savings institution offering savings products (Premium Bonds, savings certificates, income bonds) to retail investors. One of the largest savings organisations in the UK, with over 25 million customers.
NATIONAL SAVINGS AND INVESTMENTS.b: Over 25 million customers holding Premium Bonds and other NS&I products, and the government through low-cost borrowing.

UNITED KINGDOM ATOMIC ENERGY AUTHORITY P.d: Pension payments for former employees of the UK Atomic Energy Authority, covering staff who worked on the UK's nuclear research and development programmes.
UNITED KINGDOM ATOMIC ENERGY AUTHORITY P.b: Retired former UKAEA employees and their dependents.

OFFICE FOR STANDARDS IN EDUCATION, CHILD.d: Ofsted, the independent inspectorate responsible for inspecting and regulating schools, colleges, childcare providers, children's social care, and teacher training in England.
OFFICE FOR STANDARDS IN EDUCATION, CHILD.b: All pupils, students, and families through quality assurance of education and children's services. Inspects approximately 24,000 schools and 80,000 childcare providers.

COMPETITION AND MARKETS AUTHORITY.d: The UK's primary competition and consumer protection authority, investigating mergers, enforcing competition law, and protecting consumers from unfair practices. Gained new powers post-Brexit including digital markets regulation.
COMPETITION AND MARKETS AUTHORITY.b: UK consumers and businesses through fair competition, merger control, and consumer protection enforcement.

FOOD STANDARDS AGENCY.d: Independent government department responsible for food safety and food hygiene across England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Oversees food labelling, food fraud prevention, and ensures food is safe to eat.
FOOD STANDARDS AGENCY.b: All consumers through food safety regulation, and food businesses through hygiene standards and compliance support.

NATIONAL AUDIT OFFICE.d: The independent parliamentary body responsible for auditing central government accounts and assessing value for money of public spending. Headed by the Comptroller and Auditor General. Reports directly to Parliament.
NATIONAL AUDIT OFFICE.b: All taxpayers through scrutiny of public expenditure and promotion of good governance across government.

SERIOUS FRAUD OFFICE.d: Specialist prosecuting authority handling the most complex and serious cases of fraud, bribery, and corruption in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Has unique powers to compel evidence disclosure.
SERIOUS FRAUD OFFICE.b: The public and business community through prosecution of serious economic crime and deterrence of fraud and corruption.

PARLIAMENTARY WORKS GRANT.d: Funding for the restoration and renewal programme for the Palace of Westminster, one of the most significant heritage restoration projects in the UK. The building requires urgent work to address fire safety, asbestos, and crumbling infrastructure.
PARLIAMENTARY WORKS GRANT.b: Parliament as an institution, and the public through preservation of a Grade I listed UNESCO World Heritage Site.

HM LAND REGISTRY.d: Government agency responsible for registering ownership of land and property in England and Wales. Maintains a register of over 26 million titles. Operates as a trading fund, largely self-financing through registration fees.
HM LAND REGISTRY.b: Property owners, buyers, sellers, mortgage lenders, and legal professionals involved in property transactions.

THE NATIONAL ARCHIVES.d: The official archive and publisher for the UK Government, holding over 11 million historical government and public records dating back over 1,000 years. Also manages legislation.gov.uk.
THE NATIONAL ARCHIVES.b: Researchers, historians, genealogists, legal professionals, and the public accessing historical government records.

OFFICE OF GAS AND ELECTRICITY MARKETS.d: Ofgem, the energy regulator for Great Britain, protecting consumers by regulating the gas and electricity markets. Sets the energy price cap, oversees network companies, and promotes competition.
OFFICE OF GAS AND ELECTRICITY MARKETS.b: All domestic and business energy consumers in Great Britain through price regulation and market oversight.

OFFICE OF THE PARLIAMENTARY COMMISSIONER.d: The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO), handling complaints about government departments, NHS organisations, and other public bodies that have not been resolved through normal channels.
OFFICE OF THE PARLIAMENTARY COMMISSIONER.b: Individuals who have unresolved complaints about government services or NHS treatment.

ELECTORAL COMMISSION.d: The independent body overseeing elections and referendums in the UK, regulating political party finances, and promoting public awareness of the democratic process.
ELECTORAL COMMISSION.b: All voters and political parties through fair election administration and political finance regulation.

CHARITY COMMISSION.d: The regulator for charities in England and Wales, responsible for registering charities, ensuring they meet legal requirements, and investigating mismanagement or misconduct. Oversees approximately 170,000 registered charities.
CHARITY COMMISSION.b: The public and charitable sector through regulation of approximately 170,000 charities in England and Wales.

OFFICE OF QUALIFICATIONS AND EXAMINATION.d: Ofqual, the independent qualifications regulator for England, maintaining standards for GCSEs, A levels, vocational qualifications, and other regulated qualifications.
OFFICE OF QUALIFICATIONS AND EXAMINATION.b: Students taking regulated qualifications, schools, colleges, and employers who rely on qualification standards.

HOUSE OF COMMONS: MEMBERS.d: Expenditure related to Members of Parliament including salaries, staffing allowances, and support for constituency offices. Covers the costs of 650 elected members carrying out their parliamentary and constituency duties.
HOUSE OF COMMONS: MEMBERS.b: 650 Members of Parliament and their constituents who benefit from constituency services.

HM PROCURATOR GENERAL AND TREASURY SOLIC.d: The Government Legal Department, providing legal advice and litigation services to government departments. The Treasury Solicitor acts as the government's principal legal adviser and handles Bona Vacantia (ownerless property).
HM PROCURATOR GENERAL AND TREASURY SOLIC.b: Government departments receiving legal services, and the public through proper legal governance of government activities.

UNITED KINGDOM SUPREME COURT.d: The highest court of appeal in the United Kingdom for civil cases (and criminal cases from England, Wales, and Northern Ireland). Established in 2009 to replace the Appellate Committee of the House of Lords. Hears approximately 60-80 cases per year.
UNITED KINGDOM SUPREME COURT.b: Parties in cases of major public or constitutional importance, and the wider legal system through precedent-setting judgments.

LOCAL GOVERNMENT BOUNDARY COMMISSION FOR.d: Independent body responsible for conducting reviews of local authority boundaries, ward boundaries, and council sizes in England to ensure electoral equality and effective local government.
LOCAL GOVERNMENT BOUNDARY COMMISSION FOR.b: Local government voters and councils through fair and up-to-date electoral boundaries.

CROWN ESTATE OFFICE.d: The Crown Estate manages a GBP 16 billion portfolio of property and land owned by the monarch 'in right of the Crown', including Regent Street, much of the seabed around England/Wales/NI, and offshore wind lease areas. Net revenue is paid to the Treasury.
CROWN ESTATE OFFICE.b: The Treasury (and thus all taxpayers) through net revenue surplus, plus offshore wind developers leasing seabed areas.

EXPORT CREDITS GUARANTEE DEPARTMENT.d: UK Export Finance (UKEF), the UK's export credit agency, providing government-backed loans, insurance, and guarantees to support UK exports. Helps UK companies win overseas contracts that might not proceed without government support.
EXPORT CREDITS GUARANTEE DEPARTMENT.b: UK exporters and their overseas buyers, particularly in sectors like defence, aerospace, infrastructure, and clean energy.

GOVERNMENT ACTUARY'S DEPARTMENT.d: Provides actuarial advice to government on public sector pension schemes, social security, insurance regulation, and risk management. Advises on the financial sustainability of major government liabilities.
GOVERNMENT ACTUARY'S DEPARTMENT.b: Government departments making decisions on pension scheme design, insurance regulation, and long-term fiscal planning.

OFFICE OF RAIL AND ROAD.d: The independent safety and economic regulator for the railway industry in Great Britain, and the monitor of Highways England. Ensures railway safety, sets track access charges, and monitors Network Rail's performance.
OFFICE OF RAIL AND ROAD.b: Rail passengers, freight operators, and road users through safety regulation and performance monitoring of rail and road networks.

WATER SERVICES REGULATION AUTHORITY.d: Ofwat, the economic regulator of the water and sewerage sectors in England and Wales. Sets price limits for water companies, monitors their performance, and protects consumer interests. Has faced increased scrutiny over sewage spills.
WATER SERVICES REGULATION AUTHORITY.b: All water and sewerage customers in England and Wales through price regulation and service quality standards.
